image_prompt: SVG: Distance / km (0–4.0) vs Time of day (07:55–08:25), V-shaped line falling 4→1 km then rising back to 3 km; points at each 5-min interval.

render_data.title: Choose the correct line of best fit
render_data.prompt: The graph shows another student's results — average bounce height / m (y, 0–0.80) against internal air pressure / kPa (x, 0–50), with five rising data points: (30, 0.59), (35, 0.62), (40, 0.65), (45, 0.68), (50, 0.71). Select the option that best describes a correct line of best fit for these points.
options: A straight line passing through (or as close as possible to) all five points — about 0.41 m at 0 kPa rising to about 0.71 m at 50 kPa
A straight line through the origin (0, 0), rising steeply to the data
A curve that bends sharply upward through the last two points only
A horizontal line at 0.65 m across every pressure value
render_data.correct: A straight line passing through (or as close as possible to) all five points — about 0.41 m at 0 kPa rising to about 0.71 m at 50 kPa

topic: Bounce height vs drop height scatter

render_data.title: Label the axes of the bounce-height graph
render_data.prompt: The mean data from the experiment is plotted with unlabelled axes. Drag the correct label into the x-axis (horizontal) slot and the y-axis (vertical) slot. (You would then plot your value from part (b), the point (1.8, 0.51), on the grid.)

topic: Lightning conductor on a tall building
image_prompt: SVG: a tiled-roof tower with a metal lightning-conductor rod at the apex and a down-conductor strap running to earth; labels 'Lightning conductor' and 'to earth'.

topic: Alpha/beta decay equations with draggable particles

render_data.title: Select the particle released in each decay
render_data.prompt: Drop each decay equation into the box for the particle it releases. (Alpha ⁴₂α lowers the mass number by 4 and the atomic number by 2; beta ⁰₋₁β keeps the mass number the same and raises the atomic number by 1.)

image_prompt: Data table: Isotope | Half-life / years | Additional information — Curium-242 / 4 730 / artificially produced, not found in nature; Carbon-14 / 5 700 / alongside carbon-12, found in organic materials; Potassium-40 / 1 300 000 000 / found in sedimentary rocks from volcanic dust; Uranium-238 / 4 500 000 000 / found as an ore bonded to other elements.

render_data.title: Classify the variables in the football-bounce experiment
render_data.prompt: Drop each variable into the correct column: the one factor you change (independent), the one you measure (dependent), or those you keep constant (control).
